description:
You are acting as a technical-writer who keeps all project documentation under
    /home/user/docs.  
    Your goal is to make sure that a small maintenance script called
    /home/user/scripts/build_docs.sh is executed automatically every weekday
    (Monday–Friday) at 09:30 in the morning and that any console output (STDOUT
    or STDERR) produced by the script is appended to an existing log file
    /home/user/logs/build_docs.log.

    1. Create the cron job in *your own* user crontab (do **not** touch
       /etc/cron.d or system-wide crontabs).  
       The cron entry must be exactly:

           30 09 * * 1-5 /home/user/scripts/build_docs.sh >> /home/user/logs/build_docs.log 2>&1

       • No leading or trailing spaces.  
       • Do **not** add any extra comment lines.  
       • There must be no duplicate copies of this line in the resulting crontab.

    2. After installing the job, capture the *entire* contents of your crontab
       by running a single command that redirects the output of `crontab -l`
       into /home/user/logs/crontab_after_task.txt.

       Required format of /home/user/logs/crontab_after_task.txt:
       • Plain text.  
       • Every line must be exactly as `crontab -l` prints it.  
       • The file must contain only the cron lines that are currently active
         (no explanatory sentences from you).

    The automated tests will check both that the crontab really contains the
    specified entry and that /home/user/logs/crontab_after_task.txt exists with
    the correct contents.  You do not need to execute the script itself—only
    schedule it and save the crontab snapshot.

    IMPORTANT:
    • Achieve everything with normal user-level permissions.  
    • A single compound shell command is sufficient, but feel free to break the
      work into multiple commands if you prefer.  
    • Do **not** display confidential data or modify unrelated files.
truth:
Expected state after successful completion:

    1. User crontab (`crontab -l`) contains one and only one active (non-comment)
       line:
           30 09 * * 1-5 /home/user/scripts/build_docs.sh >> /home/user/logs/build_docs.log 2>&1

    2. The file /home/user/logs/crontab_after_task.txt exists, is world-readable
       (644 or stricter), and its complete contents are exactly:

           30 09 * * 1-5 /home/user/scripts/build_docs.sh >> /home/user/logs/build_docs.log 2>&1

       followed by a single trailing newline (no extra blank lines).

    3. No other files are created or modified in /home/user/logs/ or elsewhere
       except those mentioned above.

    4. Directory /home/user/logs/ already exists before the task starts with
       proper write permissions for the user.

    The verification script will:
       a. run `crontab -l` and grep for the exact cron string, ensuring only one
          match exists;
       b. inspect /home/user/logs/crontab_after_task.txt for exact match (byte
          for byte) to the expected line plus newline;
       c. confirm that no duplicate entries are present.
